title: Extracting structural Info for schematics
body:
Short preface:
I am thinking about starting an effort for some kind of open-source schematic tracer for digital, synthesizable logic.
I feel that this is something that is substantially lacking in open source EDA-tooling right now.
Gtkwave is great, but in some cases looking at a structural schematic just makes it easier to see what is going on.
(e.g. when debugging CDC violations)

I was considering to build on the V/SV-capabilities of verilator here as it follows a very 'synthesis-like' approach and also has linting capabilities, which should make it aware of everything that a schematic representation needs.

My Question: I am not an expert of verilator internals, intermediate repesentations that might exist etc.

So, could you give me some guidance at which layer I should be looking at. I have different possibilities for tackling this in mind, each of which might have different advantages and disadvantages:

a) (Abstract/Concrete) Syntax Trees: These could generate the set of 'primitives' in the schematic. (modules, assignments/logical operators, processes etc.)
b) Connection Info: Extracting this from just Syntax is painful, if OOM references are used it is actually impossible. How does verilator represent this?
c) Structural Info: multiple drivers, clock domain of a signal etc. - Here some info must be available too, otherwise linting wouldn't work.

Grateful for any ideas on the subject.
